name: python-venv-manager
description: Python virtual environment management, dependency handling, and project setup automation.
qualityReason: Useful but overlaps with env tools
capabilities: Create and configure Python virtual environments
Manage dependencies with Poetry, pip-tools, and pipenv
Set up Python version management with pyenv
Troubleshoot dependency conflicts and installation issues
Configure development environments with testing and linting
author: CuriousLearner
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing educational content about Python virtual environments. No executable code, scripts, or network operations are present. All 150 static findings are false positives triggered by bash command examples demonstrating legitimate Python development workflows (poetry, pipenv, pyenv installation and usage). The skill explicitly warns against unsafe practices such as sudo pip install and committing .env files.
valueStatement: Struggling with Python dependency conflicts and virtual environment setup? This skill provides expert guidance on creating, managing, and troubleshooting Python virtual environments using modern tools like Poetry, pipenv, and pyenv.
limitations: Requires existing Python installation on system
Some tools (Poetry, pyenv) need separate installation
Platform-specific commands may vary (Windows vs Linux/Mac)
Cannot directly execute commands - provides instructions only

useCases.title: New Project Setup
useCases.description: Create properly structured Python projects with virtual environments and dependency management from scratch.

useCases.title: Dependency Management
useCases.description: Standardize dependency management across team with lock files and reproducible environments.

useCases.title: Environment Troubleshooting
useCases.description: Diagnose and fix Python environment issues, version conflicts, and broken dependencies.

promptTemplates.title: Create New Project
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting a Python project with Poetry
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me set up a new Python project using Poetry with Django, pytest, and black formatter. Include proper project structure and configuration.

promptTemplates.title: Fix Dependencies
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm getting dependency conflicts with requests and urllib3 versions. How do I resolve this using Poetry?

promptTemplates.title: Migrate Project
promptTemplates.prompt: I have an existing project using requirements.txt. How do I migrate it to Poetry while keeping all dependencies?

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Python Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to work with Python 3.9, 3.10, and 3.11 on different projects. Set up pyenv and configure each project with the right version.

bestPractices: Always use virtual environments - never install packages globally
Pin exact dependency versions in production using lock files
antiPatterns: Using sudo pip install or installing packages system-wide
Committing virtual environment folders to version control
Using unpinned dependencies in production environments

faq.question: Which tool should I use - venv, virtualenv, or Poetry?
faq.answer: Use venv for simple projects, Poetry for modern dependency management with lock files and publishing capabilities.

faq.question: How do I activate virtual environments on Windows?
faq.answer: Use 'venv\Scripts\activate' on Windows instead of 'source venv/bin/activate' used on Linux/Mac.

faq.question: Can I use this with existing requirements.txt files?
faq.answer: Yes, you can migrate existing projects. Use 'poetry add $(cat requirements.txt)' to import dependencies.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using dependency management tools?
faq.answer: These tools only manage Python packages. They don't access personal data or send information externally.

faq.question: What if I get 'command not found' errors?
faq.answer: Ensure the tool is installed and in your PATH. For Poetry, use the official installer script.

faq.question: How does this compare to conda environments?
faq.answer: Python venv is lighter and Python-specific. Conda handles multiple languages but is heavier and slower.
